source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Facebook's advertising revenue share was structurally undervalued in 2012 relative to its share of consumer time spent.
summary_extended: Baker argues that in 2012, Facebook was structurally undervalued because its share of advertising revenue (~4%, or ~$5B) was dramatically misaligned with its share of consumer time (~7%), and more broadly, internet/mobile commanded ~35% of consumer time but only ~23% of US ad dollars. He draws on historical precedent from radio and television, where ad dollars consistently followed consumer attention with a lag, to argue this convergence was inevitable. Baker believed Facebook's fair-share ad revenue implied roughly $35B in global ad revenue, with additional upside from the measurability premium of digital advertising and the potential for Facebook to capture CAC budgets that had previously been allocated to physical retail rent. This was a high-conviction, large position Baker executed retrospectively, and he describes it as one of the clearest structural mispricings he identified — a case where the data made the outcome feel near-certain.
detail: In 2012, internet/mobile was ~35% of consumer time but only ~23% of US ad revenue. Facebook specifically was ~7% of consumer time but only ~4% (~$5B) of ad dollars. Historical precedent (radio, TV) shows ad dollars always follow consumer attention with a lag. Fair share implied ~$35B in global ad revenue for Facebook, with upside from measurability premium and CAC replacing rent.
confidence_evidence: This is a retrospective thesis Baker executed with high conviction; described as a 'big position.'
quote_fragment: I was very confident that 23% of advertising revenue would go to 36%

ticker_audit.reasoning: The thesis is specifically and entirely about Facebook's advertising revenue undervaluation relative to consumer time share in 2012; FB is the direct subject of the investment thesis.
